title: RDG 1: Foundations of Human Development
description: Long-term societal resilience requires guaranteeing conditions for optimal physical, emotional, cognitive and relational maturation. Holistic psychological development, secure attachment, healthy neurological conditions.
problems: Education focuses only on basic literacy, neglecting holistic psychological development.
solutions: Holistic redesign of education systems; intergenerational mentoring; early childhood interventions; IDG Framework.

title: RDG 2: Trauma-Informed Societies
description: Structural violence and intergenerational wounds actively sabotage peacebuilding. Embedding trauma awareness into public policy, healthcare and justice systems.
problems: State institutions often re-traumatise the vulnerable. Collective trauma is excluded from decision-making.
solutions: SAMHSA principles in public policy; trauma-informed justice; community-level trauma processing.

title: RDG 3: Inner Development Competencies
description: The IDG framework institutionalises 23 core skills into global education and corporate training systems: Being, Thinking, Relating, Collaborating, Acting.
problems: Education neglects inner development skills, leading to tribal polarisation.
solutions: IDG framework institutionalisation; competency-based assessment; self-awareness and collaboration skills in curricula.

title: RDG 4: Leadership Maturity Standards
description: Public-sector and corporate leadership must be evaluated based on vertical development, cultivating leaders who operate from post-conventional, regenerative action logics.
problems: Current leaders act from short-term thinking, unable to dissolve their own immunity to change.
solutions: Vertically-developmental leadership programmes; maturity assessments; post-conventional logic development.

title: RDG 5: Epistemic Integrity and Sensemaking
description: The spread of algorithmic bias, automated disinformation and synthetic media threatens collective intelligence and democratic viability.
problems: False narratives, AI-generated disinformation and cognitive biases undermine democracy.
solutions: Mandatory AI transparency requirements; metacognitive competencies; protection of knowledge commons.

title: RDG 6: Regenerative Cultural Narratives
description: Systemic transformation is preceded by cultural transformation. Active movement away from extractive consumption and hyperindividualism toward responsibility and pluriversal worldviews.
problems: Dominant cultural narratives reinforce extraction and individualism.
solutions: Art and media carrying regenerative narratives; pluriversal education; Earth-stewardship identity.

title: RDG 7: Climate Stabilisation and Adaptation
description: Going beyond simple emissions reduction: massive atmospheric carbon drawdown and radical adaptive capacity. Deep climate variability is already locked into the Earth system.
problems: Carbon emission reductions alone are insufficient — massive drawdown and adaptation to extreme events are simultaneously required.
solutions: Natural climate solutions; carbon removal technologies; anticipatory climate governance; infrastructure adaptation.

title: RDG 8: Biodiversity and Habitat Restoration
description: Scaled, net-positive regeneration of terrestrial, freshwater and marine ecosystems. Creation of continental-scale ecological corridors.
problems: Habitat fragmentation, invasive species, industrial agriculture and urbanisation are drivers of the planet's sixth mass extinction.
solutions: Continental ecological corridors; rewilding programmes; extending conservation to 30% of land and ocean.

title: RDG 9: Regenerative Food and Soil Systems
description: Full transition from industrial, chemical-intensive agriculture to agroecological, perennial and soil-regenerating food systems.
problems: Industrial agriculture destroys soil by depleting organic carbon content while threatening biodiversity.
solutions: Regenerative agriculture (on at least 40% of global cropland); agroforestry; soil carbon measurement systems.

title: RDG 10: Water Cycle and Watershed Integrity
description: Shifting water management from siloed urban utility regulation toward holistic watershed stewardship.
problems: Water management is fragmented, groundwater levels are falling, and wetland degradation has accelerated.
solutions: Watershed-level governance; groundwater recharge zone restoration; river renaturalisation.

title: RDG 11: Bioregional Governance
description: Economic and political decision-making aligned with natural bioregions (watersheds, mountain ranges) rather than artificial political boundaries.
problems: Nation-state borders cut across ecosystems, leading to the tragedy of the commons.
solutions: Bioregional decision-making; strengthening local ecological-cultural identity; cross-border ecological partnerships.

title: RDG 12: Urban-Rural Ecological Integration
description: Understanding human settlements as active, functional components within the wider ecosystem. Cities must be designed to restore the natural environment of their hinterlands.
problems: Cities currently burden ecosystems rather than being part of them.
solutions: Nature-based solutions in cities; green infrastructure networks; closed-loop urban resource flows.

title: RDG 13: Basic Needs Security
description: Guaranteeing absolute baseline security — access to clean water, housing, healthcare, energy and education — as an unconditional human right.
problems: Millions' biological survival depends on unstable labour markets, creating poverty traps and vulnerability.
solutions: Unconditional basic income pilots; housing guarantee programmes (Housing First); decentralised energy provision.

title: RDG 14: Regenerative and Circular Economy
description: Shifting from the industrial linear "take-make-dispose" model to mandatory, systemic circularity. Full internalisation of all ecological and social costs into market prices.
problems: Material throughput strains planetary boundaries. External social and ecological costs are not reflected in profits.
solutions: Circular industrial systems; extended producer responsibility; ecological tax systems; Doughnut Economics.

title: RDG 15: Inclusive Livelihoods and Meaningful Work
description: Replacing the simple metric of aggregate job creation with focus on the quality, dignity and purpose of livelihoods. Economic democracy and fair income distribution.
problems: The quantitative focus on job creation obscures work quality and worker dignity.
solutions: Social enterprises; worker ownership models; fair wage standards; retraining programmes.

title: RDG 16: Resilient Infrastructure and Engineering
description: Adopting "regenerative engineering" principles for every civil, structural and digital project. Infrastructure must achieve net-positive impact.
problems: Current infrastructure design is linear and fails catastrophically in extreme weather events.
solutions: Bio-based building materials with carbon-sequestration capacity; modular and repairable systems; climate-volatility-ready design.

title: RDG 17: Responsible Technology Governance
description: Advanced technology governed as a critical part of the social commons. Aligning biotechnology, artificial intelligence and digital infrastructure with human dignity.
problems: Technological monopolisation and "digital enclosure" threaten data sovereignty and technology access.
solutions: Participatory AI regulation; data sovereignty; open-source ecosystems; ethical AI safeguards.

title: RDG 18: Distributed Renewable Energy Systems
description: Accelerating the transition to zero-carbon energy through mandatory expansion of decentralised microgrids and community-owned renewable networks.
problems: Dependence on monolithic energy monopolies makes systems fragile. The fossil energy transition is not fast enough.
solutions: Community-owned renewable energy projects; microgrid technologies; decentralised storage capacity; energy communities.

title: RDG 19: Transparent and Accountable Institutions
description: Strengthening the rule of law and restoring broken public institutional trust through radical transparency and anti-corruption guarantees.
problems: Regulatory capture, institutional corruption and lack of accountability undermine sustainable development.
solutions: Government open-data mandates; strong anti-corruption institutions; civil society involvement in oversight.

title: RDG 20: Polycentric and Participatory Governance
description: Operationalising Nobel laureate Elinor Ostrom's principles for managing the commons. Decentralising power through subsidiarity.
problems: One-size-fits-all national mandates fail to adapt to local ecological and social diversity.
solutions: Institutionalising Ostrom's principles; participatory decision-making platforms; community-developed rules.

title: RDG 21: Equity and Structural Inclusion
description: Addressing the deeper causes of social marginalisation by dismantling systemic inequalities along lines of gender, race, geography and social origin.
problems: Superficial equal opportunities are insufficient — systemic inequality destabilises society.
solutions: Targeted equity policies; redistribution of economic power; strong social floor standards.

title: RDG 22: Peacebuilding and Conflict Transformation
description: Transcending militarised security models by foregrounding restorative justice, community mediation and systemic de-escalation.
problems: Climate-change-induced resource scarcity increasingly leads to inter-group violence.
solutions: Restorative justice systems; community mediation; prevention-focused conflict management.

title: RDG 23: Open Science and the Knowledge Commons
description: Defining critical scientific knowledge, environmental data and technological innovations as non-exclusive public goods.
problems: Patent accumulation and paywalled journals prevent the rapid spread of necessary technologies.
solutions: Open-source research communities; open-access publishing standards; technology transfer to the Global South.

title: RDG 24: Feedback-Based Policy Learning
description: Introducing robust "anticipatory governance" models across all levels of the public sector. Requiring institutions to shift toward flexible, adaptive policy pathways.
problems: Rigid, long-term legislative planning does not adapt to highly uncertain futures.
solutions: Horizon-scanning systems; rapid pilot testing; iterative feedback loops; anticipatory policy frameworks.

title: RDG 25: Existential Risk Reduction
description: Integrating global catastrophic risk (GCR) reduction into development frameworks. Coordinated global protocols for risks from AI, synthetic bioweapons and nuclear proliferation.
problems: Development frameworks ignore existential risks threatening civilisational survival.
solutions: Global existential risk monitoring systems; proactive AI safety protocols; biosafety frameworks.

title: RDG 26: Intergenerational Representation
description: Legal institutionalisation of the rights and systemic representation of future generations. Decade-long impact assessments for all public-institution decisions, modelled on the Welsh Future Generations Act.
problems: Political decision-making is short-sighted, focused on electoral cycles while ignoring future generations.
solutions: Future Generations Commissioner institution; statutory long-term impact assessment obligations; legal representation of children.

title: RDG 27: Global Early Warning and Foresight Systems
description: Integrated satellite and field monitoring networks capable of identifying non-linear tipping points in both Earth systems and social systems.
problems: Systemic early warning systems for ecological tipping points and social system collapse are absent.
solutions: Integrated satellite monitoring networks; machine-learning-based early signal detection; global tipping-point dashboard.

title: RDG 28: Integrated Inner-Outer Metrics
description: Categorically replacing GDP as the primary indicator of national success with comprehensive dashboards measuring structural vulnerability, ecological integrity and human developmental maturity.
problems: GDP as a success indicator misleads decision-makers by ignoring ecological and social dimensions.
solutions: WISE framework and Genuine Progress Indicators (GPI); ecological integrity measures; complex wellbeing dashboards.

title: RDG 29: Civilisational-Scale Transition Strategy
description: Exiting the fossil, hyper-extractive era requires coordinated, gradual and conscious macroeconomic and cultural descent.
problems: Rapid shutdown of unsustainable industries can cause massive social disruption. The transition has proceeded without coordination.
solutions: Geels multi-level perspective; just transition programmes; industrial brownfield rehabilitation.

title: RDG 30: Planetary Stewardship Identity
description: The ultimate north star of the RDG framework: humanity's shift from dominance and separateness toward interconnectedness and symbiosis.
problems: Human civilisation is based on the myth of separation and dominance, which generates all other problems.
solutions: Education in planetary stewardship identity; embedding Earth-system ecology in cultural narratives.

title: Permaculture
description: Design and implementation of sustainable agricultural and lifestyle systems.
problems: Industrial agriculture and urban lifestyles have become disconnected from natural processes, leading to unsustainable resource consumption.
solutions: Learning and applying permaculture design principles; community gardens; agroforestry systems; natural building.

title: Eco-Architecture
description: Design and implementation of energy-efficient, ecosystem-friendly built environments.
problems: The building industry is one of the largest carbon emitters and resource consumers, contributing to climate change.
solutions: Natural building materials (adobe, wood, stone); passive house design; green roofs and green walls; solar-integrated design; bioclimatic principles.

title: Community Building
description: Communication culture deepening and human connection at local and global levels.
problems: In an atomised, individualistic society, people suffer from loneliness and lack of community, which weakens collective agency.
solutions: Creating community spaces; sociocratic decision-making; sharing common tools and resources; intergenerational programmes.

title: Local Economy
description: Strengthening local economic activity and money circulation, increasing community self-sufficiency and reducing local vulnerability to global capital forces.
problems: In a globalised economy, a large portion of local resources and purchasing power flows toward multinational corporations, weakening local self-determination.
solutions: Local currencies and exchange programs; community cooperatives; prioritising local products and services; community funds.

title: Education
description: Supporting lifelong learning that develops critical thinking and creativity.
problems: Traditional education focuses excessively on memorisation and testing, neglecting holistic development, connection to nature, and systems thinking.
solutions: Alternative pedagogies (Waldorf, Montessori, democratic schools); project-based learning; nature-based education; mentoring systems.

title: Health
description: An integrated approach to holistic health — physical, mental, spiritual and community wellbeing. Prevention-focused, nature-centred and accessible healthcare.
problems: Modern medicine often focuses on treating symptoms rather than nurturing the roots of health: connection to nature, diet, movement, and connection.
solutions: Integrative medicine; prevention and lifestyle medicine; naturopathy and evidence-based traditional methods; community health programs.

title: Spirituality
description: Seeking deep meaning in life and the experience of interconnectedness.
problems: Materialist culture alienates us from the deeper meaning of life and from our interconnectedness with each other and nature, leading to inner emptiness and a crisis of values.
solutions: Meditation and contemplation; time spent in nature; rituals and community ceremonies; interspiritual dialogue; psychological developmental work.

title: Renewable Energy
description: Accelerating the transition to solar, wind, hydro and other renewable energy sources.
problems: Dependence on fossil fuels makes energy more expensive, weakens local self-determination, and contributes to climate change.
solutions: Community solar power projects; wind energy cooperatives; energy efficiency renovations; energy storage and smart grids; local energy communities.

title: Universal Clean Water and Sanitation
description: Ensuring access to safe drinking water and adequate sanitation for all people — essential for a healthy and secure life.
problems: Millions of people lack access to safe drinking water and basic sanitation, leading to widespread preventable disease and high mortality rates.
solutions: Investment in decentralized water purification systems, robust watershed protection policies, and equitable global water distribution networks.

title: Sustainable Food Security and Regenerative Agriculture
description: Building agricultural systems that create a hunger-free world while restoring the health of farmland and wider ecosystems.
problems: Current industrial agricultural systems deplete soil fertility, heavily rely on chemical inputs, and leave marginalized communities vulnerable to food insecurity.
solutions: Transitioning globally to agroecological farming, eliminating systemic food waste, and strengthening local food sovereignty programs.

title: Global Biodiversity and Ecosystem Protection
description: Preserving the diversity of life on Earth — forests, oceans and natural habitats — to maintain the planet's ecological balance.
problems: Rampant habitat destruction and pollution are accelerating a mass extinction event, unbalancing critical ecosystems that sustain life.
solutions: Implementing strict international conservation zones, aggressively restoring degraded habitats, and curbing destructive industrial expansion.

title: Holistic and Prevention-Centred Health
description: Integrated support for the physical, mental and social dimensions of human health, focused on proactive prevention of illness and improving quality of life.
problems: Healthcare systems are heavily skewed toward reactive, symptom-based treatments while ignoring foundational preventative care and lifestyle factors.
solutions: Mainstreaming lifestyle medicine, making nutritious food and safe environments accessible, and integrating holistic therapies into public health frameworks.

title: Mental Health and Emotional Wellbeing
description: Destigmatising psychological wellbeing and making emotional intelligence and professional mental health support widely accessible.
problems: Systemic stress, trauma, and societal isolation are causing a global mental health crisis, compounded by stigma and severe lack of care access.
solutions: Embedding emotional intelligence in early education, ensuring universal access to trauma-informed therapy, and destigmatizing mental health issues.

title: Quality, Integral and Lifelong Education
description: Providing education that develops critical thinking, creativity and collaboration for all ages, continuously adapting to the complexity of the world.
problems: Standardized education models fail to prepare individuals for complex global challenges, stifling creativity and ignoring lifelong learning needs.
solutions: Adopting project-based, experiential learning models globally, fostering metacognition, and providing free, accessible adult education platforms.

title: Children's Rights and Safe Development
description: Guaranteeing a non-violent, loving, safe and supportive environment for every child that enables their full potential to unfold.
problems: Millions of children face violence, exploitation, and poverty, permanently damaging their cognitive and emotional development.
solutions: Strict enforcement of global child protection laws, robust early childhood intervention programs, and providing secure environments for vulnerable families.

title: Dignity in Old Age and Intergenerational Solidarity
description: Respecting the physical and emotional needs of older people, valuing their life experience and supporting their active participation in society.
problems: Aging populations are frequently marginalized, facing social isolation, inadequate care, and financial insecurity in their later years.
solutions: Developing community-integrated eldercare, guaranteeing adequate pensions, and creating spaces that encourage intergenerational exchange.

title: Clean and Renewable Energy for All
description: Transitioning humanity's energy supply to sustainable and clean sources that operate in long-term harmony with Earth's systems.
problems: Reliance on fossil fuels exacerbates the climate crisis and leaves underserved populations without reliable, affordable energy.
solutions: Accelerating the deployment of decentralized renewable microgrids and heavily subsidizing clean energy infrastructure worldwide.

title: Circular Economy and Resource Awareness
description: Operating an economic model that minimises waste and intelligently recycles materials, respecting the planet's finite resources.
problems: The linear 'take-make-dispose' economic model is exhausting planetary resources and creating insurmountable waste management crises.
solutions: Enforcing extended producer responsibility, designing products for repair and reuse, and transitioning to completely closed-loop industrial systems.

title: Social Equity and Equal Opportunity
description: Ensuring equal opportunities for flourishing and self-realisation for every individual, regardless of their circumstances of birth.
problems: Deeply entrenched systemic biases and wealth disparities prevent marginalized groups from accessing basic opportunities for advancement.
solutions: Implementing structural wealth redistribution, anti-discrimination legislation, and targeted investments in underserved communities.

title: Safe and Dignified Housing
description: Ensuring healthy, safe and affordable homes for everyone — the foundation of human dignity and stability.
problems: Skyrocketing real estate speculation and lack of social housing have led to a global crisis of homelessness and unsafe living conditions.
solutions: Implementing robust Housing First policies, rent controls, and massive public investments in sustainable, affordable green housing.

title: Human-Centred, Value-Creating Work and Fair Livelihoods
description: Building workplace cultures and systems where work not only guarantees secure income but also serves individual flourishing.
problems: Exploitative labor practices and stagnant wages trap millions in unfulfilling, precarious work that degrades their dignity and wellbeing.
solutions: Fostering worker cooperatives, mandating living wages globally, and restructuring corporate governance to prioritize worker wellbeing alongside profit.

title: Technology Ethics and Digital Wellbeing
description: Steering technological development (including artificial intelligence) in a direction that serves humanity, protects privacy and improves collective wellbeing.
problems: Unregulated technological advancements threaten data privacy, exacerbate social polarization, and displace labor without adequate safety nets.
solutions: Establishing robust international AI safety protocols, enforcing strict data sovereignty laws, and designing algorithms that prioritize mental wellbeing.

title: Peacebuilding and Non-violent, Integrative Conflict Resolution
description: Addressing social and global conflicts through empathy, deep dialogue and cooperation rather than armed violence, at a systemic level.
problems: Militarized approaches to conflict perpetuate cycles of violence, waste vast resources, and fail to address the root causes of disputes.
solutions: Mainstreaming restorative justice practices, establishing international conflict de-escalation frameworks, and defunding the global arms trade.

title: Animal Welfare and Compassionate Treatment
description: Respecting the life and dignity of all sentient beings, eliminating unnecessary suffering and promoting responsible coexistence with animals.
problems: Industrial factory farming and unregulated wildlife trade subject billions of animals to immense, systemic cruelty and suffering.
solutions: Banning concentrated animal feeding operations, enacting strict global animal welfare standards, and shifting toward plant-based food systems.

title: Open Science and Global Knowledge Sharing
description: Making humanity's collective knowledge, scientific discoveries and innovations freely accessible for solving shared problems as quickly as possible.
problems: Crucial scientific research is frequently locked behind expensive paywalls or monopolized by corporate patents, slowing global innovation.
solutions: Mandating open-access publishing for all publicly funded research, creating global technology transfer hubs, and supporting open-source initiatives.

title: Cultural Heritage and Human Diversity
description: Respecting and protecting the world's diverse cultures, languages, wisdom traditions and heritage — preserving humanity's shared, irreplaceable network.
problems: Globalization and cultural hegemony are rapidly erasing indigenous languages, traditional knowledge systems, and diverse cultural identities.
solutions: Funding indigenous cultural preservation programs, integrating traditional ecological knowledge into modern education, and protecting heritage sites.

title: Disaster Preparedness and Community Resilience
description: Building the resilience of local and global communities against natural and human-caused crises, with a focus on recovery and solidarity.
problems: Increasingly severe climate events and societal shocks overwhelm underprepared communities, causing massive displacement and loss of life.
solutions: Investing in robust early warning systems, building decentralized community response networks, and designing climate-resilient infrastructure.

title: Responsible Management of Long-term Existential Risks
description: Safeguarding the life opportunities of future generations through conscious, preventive and systemic mitigation of global threats to human survival.
problems: Current governance structures are entirely reactive, lacking the mechanisms to foresee and prevent planetary-scale or existential catastrophes.
solutions: Establishing global foresight institutions, mandating strict oversight of advanced biotechnology and AI, and passing future generations legislation.

title: Planetary Awareness and Respect for Earth as a Living System
description: The practical application of the scientific and spiritual recognition that humanity is an inseparable, responsible part of a greater cosmic and ecological whole.
problems: A reductionist, anthropocentric worldview treats the Earth merely as a resource base, justifying limitless extraction and environmental destruction.
solutions: Fostering ecocentric education, integrating Earth jurisprudence into legal systems, and shifting cultural narratives toward planetary stewardship.
